title.en: Reference Counting & the Cyclic Garbage Collector

description.en: How CPython actually frees memory: every object counts the references pointing at it, and the instant that count hits zero the object is destroyed. Learn what raises and lowers a refcount, why sys.getrefcount reads 'one too high', why reference cycles leak, and how the generational gc and weakref clean them up.

story_variants.en:
## Memory That Frees Itself

In C you call `free()`. In Java a background collector eventually sweeps. CPython does something more immediate and more elegant: **reference counting.** Every object on the heap carries a small integer — its *refcount* — recording how many references currently point at it. The moment that number drops to **zero**, nothing can ever reach the object again, so CPython destroys it *right there*, synchronously. That is why `__del__` so often fires the instant a variable goes out of scope: deallocation isn't deferred, it's deterministic.

### What Moves the Counter

A reference is created whenever a new *name* or *container slot* starts pointing at the object, and destroyed when one stops. You don't manage this by hand — the interpreter does it for you on ordinary operations.

| Operation | Effect on refcount | Why |
|-----------|--------------------|-----|
| `b = a` (aliasing) | **+1** | a second name now references the same object |
| `lst.append(a)` | **+1** | the list slot holds a reference |
| `func(a)` (call) | **+1** (temporary) | the parameter is a new reference while the call runs |
| `d['k'] = a` | **+1** | the dict value slot references it |
| `del b` | **−1** | a name stops referencing it |
| `b = something_else` | **−1** | the old binding is dropped (other object gets +1) |
| function returns / scope exit | **−1** each | local names are released |
| `lst.pop()` / `lst.clear()` | **−1** | the container slot is freed |

When the running total reaches zero, the object is freed immediately and *its* references are decremented in turn — a small cascade that can free a whole structure at once.

### Why `sys.getrefcount` Reads One Too High

You can watch the counter with `sys.getrefcount(obj)`. Beginners are puzzled that a brand-new object reports `2`, not `1`. The reason is simple and worth remembering: **to pass `obj` into `getrefcount`, Python binds it to the function's parameter — that argument is itself a reference.** So the count you read always includes the temporary `+1` for the call. The fix in practice: never trust the *absolute* number; measure the **delta** before and after an operation. A delta is stable across Python versions and runs even when the absolute value isn't.

### The One Thing Refcounting Can't Do: Cycles

Refcounting has a fatal blind spot. Consider two objects that point at each other:

```python
a = {}
b = {}
a['peer'] = b   # b's refcount: 2
b['peer'] = a   # a's refcount: 2
del a
del b           # both names gone...
```

After `del a` and `del b`, *you* can't reach either dict — but each still holds a reference to the other, so **neither refcount ever reaches zero.** Pure refcounting would leak them forever. This is a **reference cycle.**

### Enter the Cyclic Garbage Collector

To plug exactly this hole, CPython adds a second mechanism: the **generational cyclic garbage collector** in the `gc` module. Periodically it finds groups of objects that reference *each other* but are unreachable from your program, and reclaims them. It's *generational* because most objects die young, so freshly created objects are scanned far more often than long-lived survivors — a cheap optimization. The takeaway: refcounting does the bulk of the work instantly; the cyclic gc is the safety net for the cases refcounting provably cannot handle.

### Referencing Without Owning: `weakref`

Sometimes you want to point at an object *without* keeping it alive — a cache, a parent back-pointer, an observer list. A **weak reference** does exactly that: it lets you reach an object but does **not** increment its refcount. When every *strong* reference is gone, the object is freed and the weakref goes dead (returns `None`). Using `weakref` to break the back-edge of a would-be cycle lets plain refcounting clean up immediately, no gc pass required.

> **Key idea:** CPython frees memory the instant an object's reference count hits zero — deterministic and immediate. The one case refcounting cannot solve is a reference *cycle*, where objects keep each other alive; the generational `gc` exists solely to collect those, and `weakref` lets you reference an object without owning it.

animation_sequence.code:
import sys
obj = object()
base = sys.getrefcount(obj)
alias = obj
after = sys.getrefcount(obj)
print(after - base)
del alias
print(sys.getrefcount(obj) - after)

executionSteps.description: Absolute count is 'one too high' because the argument is itself a reference.
Aliasing binds a second name to the SAME object: +1 reference.
Delta = +1. We compare deltas, not absolutes, so it's deterministic.
del removes a name: -1. The counter falls back.

left.label: Normal object: del drops refcount to 0 -> freed instantly

right.label: Cycle a<->b: del both names, refcounts stay at 1 -> leaks without gc

description.en: Refcounting frees the left case immediately. The right case (a references b, b references a) keeps both alive even when unreachable — that is exactly what the cyclic gc and weakref are for.

output: Refcount hits zero -> object freed, right now.
Cycles never hit zero -> the generational gc collects them.
weakref: point at an object without keeping it alive.

instruction.en: Make reference counting observable. Complete refcount_deltas() so it reports how an object's refcount CHANGES (the delta) for three operations on the same object: (1) binding a second name (alias), (2) appending it to a list, (3) deleting the alias name. Because absolute refcounts vary by Python build, you must report DELTAS, not absolute counts. Binding a new name adds exactly one reference, appending to a container adds one, and del removes one. With the given code the expected output is exactly: alias:1 append:1 del:-1

practice_challenges.starter_code:
import sys

def refcount_deltas():
    obj = object()
    base = sys.getrefcount(obj)

    # 1) Bind a SECOND name to the same object (aliasing):
    alias = obj
    after_alias = sys.getrefcount(obj)

    # 2) Append the SAME object to a list:
    bucket = []
    bucket.append(obj)
    after_append = sys.getrefcount(obj)

    # 3) Delete the alias name:
    del alias
    after_del = sys.getrefcount(obj)

    # Return the DELTA for each step (after - before for that step):
    return {
        "alias": after_alias - base,        # how much did binding 'alias' change it?
        "append": after_append - after_alias, # how much did the append change it?
        "del": after_del - after_append,     # how much did 'del alias' change it?
    }

r = refcount_deltas()
print(f"alias:{r['alias']} append:{r['append']} del:{r['del']}")

en: A refcount delta is just (count_after - count_before) for one operation. Comparing deltas (not absolute counts) is what makes the result deterministic across runs and Python versions.
Binding a new name (alias = obj) makes one more reference point at the SAME object, so its refcount goes UP by 1.
list.append(obj) stores a reference in a container slot: +1. del alias removes a name: -1.
Don't worry that sys.getrefcount reads 'one too high' — the extra +1 from passing obj as an argument cancels out when you subtract two readings.

practice_challenges.test_code:
r = refcount_deltas()
assert r['alias'] == 1, f"aliasing should add exactly 1 reference, got {r['alias']}"
assert r['append'] == 1, f"append should add exactly 1 reference, got {r['append']}"
assert r['del'] == -1, f"del should remove exactly 1 reference, got {r['del']}"
assert f"alias:{r['alias']} append:{r['append']} del:{r['del']}" == 'alias:1 append:1 del:-1'

q.en: In CPython, when is an object's memory primarily reclaimed?

en: Once per second on a fixed timer
The instant its reference count drops to zero
Only when the program exits
When you manually call free() on it

q.en: You create a fresh object and call sys.getrefcount(obj), expecting 1 but getting 2. Why?

en: Python always pre-allocates two copies of every object
Passing obj as the argument to getrefcount is itself a temporary reference, adding 1
The garbage collector is holding a reference
It is a bug in sys.getrefcount

q.en: Why can pure reference counting leak a reference cycle (a points to b, b points to a)?

en: Cycles are illegal in Python and crash the interpreter
Even after all external names are deleted, each object still references the other, so neither refcount ever reaches zero
Reference counting only works on lists
The objects are too large to count

q.en: What does a weakref let you do?

en: Reference an object WITHOUT incrementing its refcount, so it can still be freed
Make an object impossible to delete
Double an object's reference count for safety
Force the garbage collector to run immediately
